rationale: The page provides detailed information on the Governor's discretionary powers regarding the reservation of bills and judicial review, as well as the election procedure and constitutional provisions for the Office of the Vice President of India.
cleanedText:
• - against Chief Justice of a High Court acting in a judicial capacity. ### Governor's Powers and Discretion regarding Bills | Provision | Description | | :--- | :--- | | Governor can’t reserve re-enacted Bill | Governor may reserve a Bill only once; after re-enactment, it cannot be reserved again unless materially altered. | | No absolute discretion under Article 200 | Governor is bound by Council of Ministers’ advice except in constitutionally specified cases:
• HC powers,
• Articles 288 (taxes on water or electricity),
• Article 360 (Financial Emergency),
• Article 254(2) (Concurrent List),
• Article 304(b) (Interstate trade and commerce),
• Articles 31A/31C
• Unconstitutional Bills. | | Discretion subject to judicial review | While assent itself isn’t justiciable, withholding or reserving assent can be challenged in courts. | | President & Supreme Court consultation (Art. 143) | On Bills reserved for unconstitutionality, President should seek SC’s advisory opinion on constitutionality; opinion has strong persuasive value. | Note: Under Article 361 (1), Governor of a State is not answerable to any court for exercise and performance of powers and duties of his/her office. However, actions of government remain open to judicial review. Article 361(2) provides absolute immunity to the Governor from criminal proceedings while in office. ## 1.3. OFFICE OF VICE PRESIDENT ### Why in the news? Recently, C.P. Radhakrishnan elected as the 15th Vice-President of India. ### Election Procedure of Vice President: • Nomination: Requires 20 proposers and 20 seconders; ₹15,000 deposit is mandatory. • Voting: Conducted in Parliament House using special ECI-supplied pens. First preference vote is compulsory. • Supervision: ECI appoints the Returning Officer (Secretary General, Rajya Sabha) and observers. • Key Safeguards followed in Elections: - Provisions of the Anti-Defection Law are not applicable. - Violation of secrecy or use of unauthorized pen invalidates the vote. - Bribery or undue influence is grounds for election challenge under Presidential and Vice-Presidential Elections Act, 1952. Note: 39th Amendment to Constitution removed the authority of Supreme Court to adjudicate petitions regarding elections of President, Vice-President, Prime Minister and Speaker of Lok Sabha. Instead, a body constituted by Parliament would be vested with power to resolve such election disputes. ### Constitutional Provisions related to Vice-President of India About Office of Vice President: • Second-highest constitutional office in country. • Constitution does not specify who performs Vice President’s duties when office falls vacant. | Articles | Description | | :--- | :--- | | Article 63 | There shall be a Vice President of India. |
